title: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
summary: The Austrian composer who toured Europe as a child prodigy and then, in a short and crowded life, poured out operas, symphonies, and concertos of such grace, balance, and feeling that he became the very model of natural musical genius — and whose early death in 1791 bred the lasting myths of a poisoning rival and a pauper's grave, neither of which the evidence supports.
hook: By the age of six, Mozart was already a touring marvel, playing for empresses and kings while his father carried him across Europe like a wonder of the age. By thirty-five he was dead, leaving more than six hundred catalogued works and an unfinished Requiem on his desk. Into that short life he packed operas, symphonies, concertos, and chamber music that listeners have never stopped loving. His death in 1791 then bred two stories the world clung to: that a jealous rival named Salieri poisoned him, and that he was flung into a pauper's grave. Both are false. The play and film 'Amadeus' fixed the murder legend in modern memory, but no good evidence supports it, and Mozart most likely died of illness. His grave was a plain common one, ordinary for non-aristocratic Vienna at the time, not a disgraced mass pit. The real Mozart was neither murdered nor an outcast. He was a working master who made perfection sound easy.
associatedWorks: Symphony No. 40 in G minor, K. 550
Symphony No. 41 in C major, K. 551 (Jupiter)
Piano Concerto No. 20 in D minor, K. 466
Piano Concerto No. 23 in A major, K. 488
Clarinet Concerto in A major, K. 622
Serenade for Winds in B-flat major, K. 361 (Gran Partita)
String Quartets dedicated to Haydn, K. 387–465
Sinfonia Concertante for Violin and Viola, K. 364
The Abduction from the Seraglio, K. 384

beginner.theFigure:
Mozart could play music before most children can read. Born in Salzburg in 1756, he showed a rare gift while still a toddler. His father, Leopold, was a working musician who saw at once what his son could do. Leopold trained him hard, day after day, and then put him on the road.

For years the family traveled across Europe. Young Wolfgang played for emperors, kings, and crowds in great cities. He could perform difficult pieces at sight, name any note he heard, and make up new music on the spot. People treated him like a living wonder. His older sister Nannerl was gifted too, and she toured with him as a child.

But a child star has to grow up. As a young man, Mozart worked for the Archbishop of Salzburg, his hometown ruler. He hated being treated like a servant. So he did something risky for his time. He quit, moved to Vienna, and tried to live by his own music, teaching pupils, giving concerts, and selling what he wrote. He became one of the first great composers to strike out on his own this way.
beginner.bodyOfWork:
Mozart wrote in almost every kind of music of his day, and he did it astonishingly well. His operas are among his greatest gifts to the world. In 'The Marriage of Figaro,' clever servants outwit their master. In 'Don Giovanni,' a charming villain is finally dragged down to his doom. In 'The Magic Flute,' a fairy-tale quest mixes comedy, wonder, and deep feeling. Mozart gave even servants and rogues real inner lives, with music that made you feel what they felt.

He also wrote dozens of symphonies, big works for orchestra. He wrote piano concertos, where a single piano trades phrases with the whole band, and Mozart often played the solo part himself. He wrote string quartets, songs, and music for church.

All of it shares a special quality. The melodies are clear and beautiful, the parts are perfectly balanced, and nothing feels forced. This was the heart of what is called the Classical style: clean, graceful, and full of song. It can sound easy and light. But under that smooth surface there is real depth, real sadness, and real joy. People who think they hear only prettiness usually have not listened closely enough.
beginner.howBeenRead:
Mozart seemed to compose as if music simply poured out of him. Stories tell of whole pieces arriving in his head, almost complete, ready to be written down. He worked fast, and he worked a lot. In a life of only thirty-five years, he finished more than six hundred works.

This fluency amazed people, then and now. He could juggle several projects at once. He could write a tricky concerto in days. He seemed to make hard things look easy, the way a great athlete makes a difficult move look natural.

The truth is a little more human. Mozart did have a remarkable memory and a quick mind. But he also studied other composers closely, especially the great Bach, whose music he admired and learned from. And his own letters and papers show that he sometimes sketched, revised, and reworked his music. The gift was real. So was the labor behind it. The ease you hear is partly the result of hidden effort, which is exactly why it sounds so effortless.
beginner.whyItEndured:
Mozart died in Vienna in 1791, only thirty-five years old. He was writing a Requiem, a piece of music for the dead, and he never finished it. Someone else completed it later. His early death shocked people and quickly grew into legend.

Two stories took hold. The first says a rival composer named Antonio Salieri poisoned Mozart out of jealousy. This is not true. There is no good evidence for it. An illness, not a murderer, almost certainly ended his life. The poisoning tale spread far thanks to a famous play and the film 'Amadeus,' but it is fiction, not history.

The second story says Mozart was thrown into a pauper's grave, dumped like a beggar with no one to mourn him. This is misleading. Mozart did die in debt. But he had earned good money in his best years. He was buried in a plain, shared grave because that was the normal, money-saving custom in Vienna then for ordinary people, not a mark of shame. He was not a destitute outcast. He was a middle-class man given an ordinary burial.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Today Mozart is counted among the very greatest composers who ever lived. With Haydn and Beethoven, he helped define the Classical style. His name has become a kind of shorthand for natural genius. People who know nothing else about music know that calling someone 'a Mozart' means calling them a born talent.

But the legend can twist the real man. He was not a careless child-spirit who scribbled masterpieces without thinking. He was a hard worker who knew his craft inside out. He could be playful and rude, and he loved jokes. He also struggled with money and with bosses he found stifling.

The myths of murder and the pauper's grave grew because his death felt too sad and too early to be simple. People wanted a villain or a tragedy to explain it. The plain facts are quieter. A brilliant man got sick and died young, in debt but not disgraced, leaving behind music the world has never let go. Seeing the real Mozart means trading the dark legend for something truer and, in its way, more moving.

general.theFigure:
Picture a small boy lifted onto a bench at the keyboard in a palace hall, his feet not reaching the floor, playing for an empress while courtiers murmur. That was Mozart's childhood. Born in Salzburg in 1756, he was trained from infancy by his father Leopold, a skilled violinist and teacher who recognized a once-in-a-generation talent and organized the family's life around it.

From the age of six, Mozart toured the courts of Europe as a sensation, performing in Munich, Vienna, Paris, London, and the cities of Italy. He absorbed every musical style he encountered along the way, the Italian operatic line, the French manner, the German seriousness. The child marvel was also a sponge.

The harder story is what happened when the wonder grew up. Mozart spent unhappy years employed by the Archbishop of Salzburg, where a court musician ranked little above the kitchen staff. In 1781 he broke with the Archbishop and settled in Vienna, choosing to live as an independent composer rather than a salaried servant. He taught, performed, took commissions, and published his work. This was a precarious way to live, and it placed him among the first great composers to attempt a freelance career in the spirit of the age he embodied, an age this entry treats elsewhere.
general.bodyOfWork:
Mozart worked supremely in nearly every form available to his time, and the breadth is part of the achievement. His operas stand at the center. In 'The Marriage of Figaro' and 'Don Giovanni,' both written with the librettist Lorenzo Da Ponte, he fused glorious melody with sharp human comedy and genuine drama. A scheming servant, a faithless seducer, a wronged wife, each is given music that reveals an inner life. 'The Magic Flute,' a German-language fairy tale full of trials and wonder, ranges from low comedy to solemn ritual without breaking apart.

Beyond the stage, his instrumental music defined the Classical style. He wrote more than forty symphonies, capped by the dark G minor and the radiant 'Jupiter,' with its dazzling final movement. His piano concertos, many written for himself to perform, set a solo voice against the orchestra in a kind of conversation that balances brilliance with depth of feeling. He left string quartets, serenades, sacred works, and chamber pieces of comparable quality.

What unites all of it is a particular sound, clear, balanced, melodically rich, and far deeper than its surface ease suggests. The Classical ideal prized clarity and proportion, and Mozart met that ideal so completely that the effort behind the music disappears. The danger is to mistake the polish for shallowness. Under the grace runs real grief, real comedy, and real unease.
general.howBeenRead:
Mozart has long carried the reputation of miraculous fluency, the composer for whom music seemed to arrive whole. Contemporaries marveled at how quickly he worked and how much he produced, more than six hundred works in a life of thirty-five years. A famous letter, once taken as proof that he composed entire pieces in his head before writing a note, helped fix this image of effortless inspiration.

The picture is partly true and partly a flattering simplification. Mozart did possess an extraordinary memory and could plainly carry complex music in his mind. He worked with remarkable speed under deadline. But the letter most often quoted is now widely regarded as not authentically his, and his surviving sketches and drafts show a composer who tried things, discarded them, and revised. The fluency was real, yet it rested on relentless early training and on close study of other masters.

That study matters. In Vienna in the early 1780s, Mozart encountered manuscripts of Bach and Handel and absorbed their counterpoint, the older art of weaving independent lines together, into his own later style. The supposedly natural genius was also a serious student of the tradition. The effortlessness audiences hear is the visible tip of a long, mostly hidden discipline.
general.whyItEndured:
Mozart died in Vienna on 5 December 1791, at thirty-five, while at work on a Requiem he left unfinished. The combination of youth, genius, and a death-mass on the desk was almost designed to breed legend, and it did. Two stories in particular have proved nearly impossible to kill, and both deserve to be set straight.

The first is the poisoning. The claim that the rival composer Antonio Salieri murdered Mozart out of jealousy has no reliable evidentiary basis. Salieri and Mozart were professional rivals, not mortal enemies, and the murder story grew after both men were dead. It was dramatized in Alexander Pushkin's verse and then, far more powerfully, in Peter Shaffer's play 'Amadeus' and the 1984 film made from it. That version is gripping fiction. The medical consensus is that Mozart most likely died of an acute illness, with rheumatic fever among the leading candidates, though the exact cause cannot now be fixed with certainty.

The second is the pauper's grave. Mozart did die in debt, and his burial was obscure. But the image of a destitute outcast flung into a mass pit is misleading. He had earned well in his strong years, and his simple, unmarked common grave reflected ordinary Viennese practice of the period, shaped by cost-saving burial rules introduced under the reforms of Emperor Joseph II, not disgrace or destitution. He received a normal middle-class burial, not a beggar's. The man behind both myths was neither murdered nor cast out.
general.contestedInheritance:
Mozart left more than six hundred works in barely three decades, and he is now ranked among the greatest composers in history. With Haydn, his friend and elder, and Beethoven, who came after, he is treated as a definer of the Classical style, the supreme melodist and a master of opera. His name has passed into ordinary speech as a byword for natural genius, the talent that seems to need no labor.

That last phrase is where the legend distorts. The image of Mozart as an eternal child, a divine idiot through whom music simply flowed, owes much to later storytelling and little to the documented man. He was a disciplined professional who had trained since infancy, studied his predecessors closely, and fought hard for his independence and his income. The childish, scatological humor recorded in his letters is real, but it sat alongside a formidable craft.

The death-myths survive for a reason worth naming. An early death that ends an unfinished Requiem invites a story larger than illness and debt. A jealous murderer or a tragic pauper's end gives the loss a shape and a villain. Strip those away and the truth is quieter but not smaller. A working master fell ill and died young, in debt yet not disgraced, having made music of such balance and feeling that the world has never stopped returning to it. Holding the documented craftsman and the towering reputation together, without collapsing either into the myth, is the honest way to receive him.

advanced.theFigure:
The central interpretive trap in Mozart reception is the figure of the divine natural, the genius as conduit rather than worker, and it has distorted the subject for two centuries. Maynard Solomon's biography did much to dismantle it, reading the documentary record against the legend and exposing how thoroughly the image of the effortless child-spirit was constructed, partly by Leopold's own promotional management of his son and partly by Romantic afterthought. What the construction obscures is the most analytically interesting fact about Mozart, that the prodigy was an engineered phenomenon before it was a marketed one.

Leopold Mozart was not merely a proud father but a calculating impresario who built his son's early career as a touring spectacle, and the question this raises is uncomfortable. The skills audiences took as evidence of preternatural gift, sight-reading, modulation on demand, improvisation, were also the products of an intensive pedagogy applied to an exceptional child from his earliest years. To say this is not to deny the gift, which the later masterworks place beyond dispute, but to insist that gift and training are not separable in this case. The recurring scholarly temptation is to read the mature achievement backward as the inevitable unfolding of innate genius, when the evidence supports a more contingent account, in which an extraordinary endowment met a singular and relentless formation, and the result could have been deformed as easily as fulfilled.
advanced.bodyOfWork:
The operatic achievement poses the sharpest analytical problem, because it resists the formalist reading that serves the instrumental music well. The Da Ponte operas, 'Figaro' and 'Don Giovanni' above all, integrate music and drama so tightly that the music does psychological and even moral work that the libretto alone cannot. The ensembles are the test case. In the great finales, Mozart sustains multiple characters in simultaneous, divergent emotional states within a single coherent musical structure, so that the form itself enacts social conflict rather than merely accompanying it. Critics from the nineteenth century onward have heard in this a democratic or subversive charge, the servant Figaro given music as fully realized as his master's, and the reading is not baseless, though it risks importing later politics into a court entertainment.

'Don Giovanni' has carried the heaviest interpretive freight. Its uneasy generic status, labeled a 'dramma giocoso' yet ending in damnation, has invited readings of the Don as a proto-Romantic transgressor, an Enlightenment libertine, or a figure of pure amoral energy that the opera both relishes and punishes. Kierkegaard made the Don the emblem of the aesthetic life seducing through music itself. The danger in all such readings is to detach the work from its theatrical conventions and treat it as a philosophical statement, when its power lies precisely in holding the comic and the terrifying in unresolved tension. The music refuses to tell the listener finally whether to admire or condemn, and that refusal, rather than any thesis, is the achievement.
advanced.howBeenRead:
The myth of effortless composition rests on a textual foundation that has largely collapsed, and tracing that collapse is instructive. The so-called Rochlitz letter, long quoted as Mozart's own account of receiving whole works in his mind and merely transcribing them, is now generally judged spurious, an early-nineteenth-century fabrication that told the Romantic age what it wished to believe about genius. Once that document falls, the case for pure inspiration depends on inference from the speed and quantity of the output, which is real but proves less than it seems.

The counter-evidence is material. The surviving autographs and sketch-leaves show deletions, false starts, and reworkings inconsistent with a composer who merely copied out a finished mental score. The Köchel catalogue, the standard chronological ordering of the works first compiled by Ludwig von Köchel in 1862 and repeatedly revised since, has itself become an object of scholarly contention, because redating individual works alters the developmental narrative built upon it. What looks like a clean line of maturation is partly an artifact of how the chronology was fixed. The deeper point is that the fluency thesis and the labor thesis are not simple alternatives. Mozart composed unusually fast and revised less than many, yet the speed was the dividend of a formation so complete that much of the compositional work had been internalized in advance. The mythology errs not in noting the fluency but in mistaking its source for the supernatural rather than the trained.
advanced.whyItEndured:
The persistence of the death-myths is a case study in why false narratives outlive correction, and it rewards precise handling rather than mere debunking. The Salieri poisoning legend has no documentary support and contradicts what is known of the two composers' actual relations, which were those of competitors who also, on occasion, collaborated and praised each other. Its power is literary, not evidentiary. Pushkin's 'Mozart and Salieri,' Rimsky-Korsakov's opera on that text, and above all Shaffer's 'Amadeus' supplied a mediocrity-versus-genius drama so satisfying that audiences absorbed it as history. The legend endures because it answers a need the facts frustrate, the need for an early death to have an author and a meaning.

The pauper's-grave myth requires a finer correction, because here a true fact, debt and an obscure burial, has been inflated into a false image, destitution and disgrace. Mozart's interment in a simple, unmarked common grave was the ordinary outcome of Viennese burial regulations of the 1780s, themselves a product of Josephine reform aimed at hygiene and economy, which discouraged elaborate private graves across the social spectrum. To read that common grave as evidence of a beggar's ruin is to misread an administrative norm as a personal catastrophe. The medical question of what actually killed him remains genuinely open, with rheumatic fever, streptococcal infection, and other diagnoses all argued from the same thin retrospective evidence, and none establishable beyond reasonable doubt. The honest position holds two lines at once, firm rejection of the murder and the beggar, and frank acknowledgment that the precise cause of death cannot now be recovered.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The deepest tension in Mozart reception concerns the relation between the surface and the depth of the music, and it is not resolved by simply asserting that the depth is there. The Classical idiom Mozart perfected prizes balance, clarity, and proportion, and these are also the qualities that allowed a later age to hear the music as merely charming, decorative, even slight. The Romantic revaluation that elevated Beethoven as the heroic struggler implicitly cast Mozart as the graceful lightweight, and traces of that hierarchy persist in the cultural shorthand that makes Mozart pretty and Beethoven profound.

The scholarly recovery of Mozart's seriousness has worked to overturn this, locating the depth not against the surface but within it. On this account the very perfection of the manner is the vehicle of feeling, the grief in the G minor symphony or the unease beneath the comedy of 'Così fan tutte' carried precisely by music that never breaks decorum. The danger of this corrective is the opposite excess, a solemnizing of Mozart that loses the genuine lightness, the theatrical delight, the sheer pleasure that is also the point. 'Così fan tutte' is the hardest case, a comedy of erotic cynicism whose beauty has struck many listeners as morally troubling, too gorgeous for its cruel premise. The work refuses to resolve whether its elegance redeems or indicts its subject.

What the case finally exposes is a general problem about an art of surfaces in a culture that equates depth with difficulty and struggle. Mozart is the sharpest instance because the union of ease and feeling in his music is so complete that prying them apart, to prove the depth is real, risks destroying the very quality that makes the depth audible. The seriousness and the grace are not two things to be balanced but one thing seen from two sides, and the failure to grasp this is what keeps regenerating both the lightweight myth and the over-solemn reaction against it.

foundation.theStory: Mozart matters as the figure who became the very model of musical genius, and as a case study in how a real and extraordinary achievement gets wrapped in myth. He was a child prodigy trained and toured by his father, who grew into an adult master and one of the first great composers to live as an independent freelancer rather than a court servant. He worked supremely in opera, symphony, concerto, and chamber music, helping define the Classical style. He died in Vienna in 1791 at thirty-five, leaving an unfinished Requiem. The gap between the documented working professional and the legend of the murdered, pauper-buried divine child is the historical problem his case presents.
foundation.theDebate: The main debates are three. First, how much of the 'effortless genius' image survives scrutiny, given that the letter long cited as proof is now judged spurious and the sketches show real revision, so that fluency and labor turn out to be entangled rather than opposed? Second, how should the death be handled, since the Salieri poisoning story is fiction with no evidentiary basis, the pauper's-grave image is a true fact of debt and obscure burial inflated into a false image of disgrace, and the actual medical cause of death remains genuinely uncertain? Third, how should the relation of surface to depth be understood, given that the very clarity and grace of the Classical style let a later age dismiss Mozart as merely charming, a verdict the modern recovery of his seriousness has worked to overturn?
foundation.whyItMatters: Mozart's case opens onto questions that reach beyond music. It illuminates how the idea of the natural genius is constructed and marketed, in his case partly by a father managing a child star. It shows how compelling fiction, the play and film 'Amadeus' above all, can overwrite documented history in public memory, and how hard a satisfying false story is to dislodge. And it raises the problem of how a culture that equates depth with struggle learns to hear depth in an art of balance and ease, a question that recurs wherever surface polish is mistaken for shallowness.
questions: Mozart toured Europe from the age of six as a child marvel, trained and managed by his father Leopold. When we call him a 'natural genius,' how much are we describing an inborn gift, and how much an exceptional child shaped by relentless early training? Can the two even be separated in his case?

The story that Salieri poisoned Mozart is fiction, yet millions believe it because of a famous play and film. Why might a vivid, satisfying story defeat the documented facts in public memory, and what does that tell us about how we come to 'know' history?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Modern Mozart scholarship rests on several pillars. The documentary foundation is Otto Erich Deutsch's collection of contemporary records, which assembles the letters, reviews, and accounts against which every interpretive narrative must be checked. Maynard Solomon's biography applied a psychologically searching reading to the life, foregrounding the fraught relationship with Leopold and dismantling the sentimental image of the effortless child, though critics have questioned how far its psychoanalytic inferences outrun the evidence. Cliff Eisen and other documentary scholars have pressed the corrective further, scrutinizing which famous anecdotes and letters are actually authentic, including the now widely rejected letter describing wholesale mental composition. H.C. Robbins Landon reconstructed the texture of Mozart's final years and the circumstances of the Requiem and the death, working to separate the documented 1791 from the legend. Daniel Heartz situated Mozart within the wider Classical style alongside Haydn, treating the idiom as a shared European achievement rather than a solitary miracle. The through-line of recent work is the replacement of the inspired-genius myth with a historically grounded account of a trained, hardworking, and embedded professional.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for Mozart is unusually rich in some areas and frustratingly thin in others. A large body of music survives, much in autograph manuscripts that reveal the compositional process, alongside the chronological Köchel catalogue that orders it. The family correspondence is extensive and gives vivid access to the touring years, the break with Salzburg, and the Vienna career, though the letters are shaped by their writers' purposes and cannot be read as transparent fact. The limits bear directly on the central myths. The medical cause of death rests on retrospective reports of symptoms, with no autopsy and no remains securely identified, so that competing diagnoses such as rheumatic fever or streptococcal infection are argued from the same insufficient evidence and none can be confirmed. The exact authorship of portions of the completed Requiem, finished after Mozart's death by Süssmayr and others, cannot be fully disentangled. And the inner experience of the prodigy, what the child actually understood of his own gift, is recoverable only by inference from external record.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position on Mozart has to hold several things together without letting any collapse the others. It must treat the music as a formal achievement of the first rank, not merely an object of inherited affection, while recognizing that the Classical idiom's surface ease has repeatedly misled listeners into underrating it. It must firmly reject the Salieri murder as fiction and the pauper's-grave image as a distortion, while honestly conceding that the precise cause of death is unrecoverable, refusing the temptation to replace one confident story with another. The strongest recent scholarship does exactly this, holding the gift and the training as inseparable rather than choosing between innate genius and made craftsman. The most important analytical move is to separate the question of the music's quality, which the scores can support, from the question of the biographical legend, which the documents largely deflate, and to resist the urge to make the second carry the first. A reading that needs the murdered, pauper-buried child to feel the music's depth has misunderstood where the depth lies.
questions: Solomon reads Mozart's life through his strained relationship with his father, an interpretation some scholars find more psychologically suggestive than the evidence strictly allows. Does showing that a biographical reading is shaped by modern psychology weaken it, or can such a reading be both interpretively bold and genuinely illuminating?

The completed Requiem is partly Mozart and partly the hands that finished it after his death, and the cause of that death cannot be securely established. How should a responsible account present a work and an event so entangled with uncertainty, and where must its claims to knowledge stop?

research.topic: The Salieri poisoning story as legend, not history
research.content: The scholarly consensus is firm that Antonio Salieri did not poison Mozart and that no reliable evidence supports any murder. Mozart died in Vienna on 5 December 1791, and contemporary accounts attribute his death to acute illness rather than foul play. Salieri and Mozart were professional rivals in the competitive Viennese music world, but also at times collaborators who praised each other's work, and the relationship was not one of murderous enmity. The poisoning rumor circulated in the years after both men had died and was given enduring literary life by Alexander Pushkin's 1830 dramatic poem 'Mozart and Salieri,' set to music by Rimsky-Korsakov, and far more powerfully by Peter Shaffer's 1979 play 'Amadeus' and Miloš Forman's 1984 film. These works are dramatizations, not documentary history. The careful formulation is that the murder narrative is fiction whose hold on public memory derives from its dramatic force, not from any evidentiary basis.

Mozart was buried in a simple, unmarked common grave in Vienna, but the popular image of a destitute pauper flung into a mass pit is a distortion. He did die in debt, and his burial was modest and obscure, with no surviving marked spot. However, this reflected ordinary Viennese burial practice of the 1780s rather than disgrace or extreme poverty. Burial reforms enacted under Emperor Joseph II discouraged elaborate, costly private graves and encouraged simple communal interment across social classes for reasons of hygiene and economy. Mozart had earned substantial income during his most successful Vienna years, and his family was middle-class, not destitute. His obscure burial was therefore an unremarkable application of contemporary rules and cost-saving custom, not evidence that he died a friendless beggar. The corrected account distinguishes the genuine facts, debt and an unmarked common grave, from the false inflation of those facts into an image of social ruin.

research.topic: The precise medical cause of Mozart's death
research.content: The exact cause of Mozart's death at thirty-five cannot be securely established. What evidence would resolve it: a contemporary autopsy report, securely identified physical remains permitting modern analysis, or detailed, medically reliable clinical records of his final illness. Why that evidence is unavailable: no autopsy was performed, his remains were placed in a common grave and have never been reliably identified or recovered, and the surviving descriptions of his final illness are secondhand, brief, and recorded in the medical vocabulary of the time rather than in terms a modern diagnosis can use with confidence. What can be responsibly inferred: he most likely died of an acute illness with fever and swelling, and rheumatic fever, a streptococcal infection, and several other conditions have each been argued by later physicians and scholars from the same limited symptom reports. What must remain unknowable: which specific disease killed him, since the competing diagnoses rest on inference from insufficient retrospective evidence, and no available source can adjudicate among them with certainty. What is certain is only the negative, that there is no evidentiary basis for death by poisoning.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: The Romantic cult of the natural genius
comparativeNarrative.name: The Divine Child
comparativeNarrative.content: From the early nineteenth century, a powerful image of Mozart took hold: the eternal child through whom music flowed without effort, a being touched by the divine. This view drew on the real wonder of the prodigy years and on a letter, later judged not authentic, that described whole works arriving complete in his mind. It served the Romantic age's hunger for genius as something inborn and almost supernatural, set apart from ordinary labor. The image was not pure invention, since the gift and the fluency were real. But it flattened the documented man, a trained, hardworking professional who studied his predecessors and revised his scores. The 'divine child' tells us as much about what later audiences wanted from genius as about Mozart himself.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Working Professional
comparativeNarrative.content: Scholars working from the letters, contracts, reviews, and manuscripts have assembled a very different Mozart. This is a man who trained from infancy under a demanding father, toured as a managed spectacle, chafed against servitude in Salzburg, and then fought to make a living as an independent composer in Vienna. He negotiated fees, taught pupils, ran subscription concerts, and worried about money. The documentary record deflates the legend at almost every point, replacing the effortless spirit with a skilled craftsman embedded in the institutions and markets of his time. This account does not diminish the music. It explains the conditions that produced it, and it shows how much of the 'natural' achievement rested on relentless formation and hard professional labor that the myth conveniently forgets.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Tragic Legend
comparativeNarrative.content: For much of the public, Mozart is known less through the music than through the story of his death: the jealous rival, the poison, the unfinished Requiem, the pauper's grave. The play and film 'Amadeus' fixed this version in modern memory, pitting envious mediocrity against careless genius. From this angle, what matters is the emotional shape of the tale, an early death given a villain and a meaning. The trouble is that the shape is false. There was no poisoning, and the obscure grave was ordinary custom, not disgrace. Yet the legend's grip reveals something real about how audiences receive artists, the wish for a great loss to carry a story larger than illness and debt. Correcting the myth means giving up a satisfying drama for a quieter truth.

comparativeSummary: Three accounts of Mozart sit beside one another, and the work of understanding him is largely the work of holding them together. The Romantic cult of the divine child captured the genuine wonder of the prodigy but inflated it into a myth of effortless, supernatural gift. The documentary scholarship that replaced it recovered a trained, hardworking professional fighting for his independence and income, without lessening the music it explains. And the death-legends that dominate popular memory, the poisoning and the pauper's grave, reveal the public's hunger to give an early death a villain and a meaning, even though the evidence dissolves both stories. No single account suffices: the gift was real, the labor was real, and the myths are false but revealing. Seeing Mozart whole means trading the dark legend for the documented man, and finding that the truth, quieter than the drama, loses none of the music's depth.

rabbitHole.label: Johann Sebastian Bach
rabbitHole.reason: Mozart encountered manuscripts of Bach and Handel in Vienna in the early 1780s and absorbed their counterpoint, the art of weaving independent lines, into his own later style. The supreme Baroque master was a model the supposedly natural genius studied with real seriousness.

rabbitHole.label: Ludwig van Beethoven
rabbitHole.reason: Beethoven built on the Classical style that Mozart and Haydn defined, then pushed it toward the Romantic era. The younger master inherited the forms Mozart raised to a peak, and the contrast between graceful Mozart and heroic Beethoven shaped how both were later heard.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: Mozart's Classical style, with its prizing of clarity, balance, and proportion, embodies the spirit of the age of reason in which he lived. The patronage world he chafed against and the freelance independence he sought both belong to that era's social transformation.

rabbitHole.label: Opera
rabbitHole.reason: Mozart raised opera to one of its heights, fusing melody with human comedy and drama in 'Figaro,' 'Don Giovanni,' and 'The Magic Flute.' Understanding the form he transformed, its conventions, ambitions, and history, deepens any account of what those works achieved.

rabbitHole.label: Romanticism
rabbitHole.reason: The Romantic movement built the cult of the suffering, inspired genius that reshaped Mozart's image into the divine child of effortless inspiration. The myths of his death and his 'natural' gift owe much to how a later age wanted to understand artistic creation.

rabbitHole.label: Vienna
rabbitHole.reason: Mozart broke with Salzburg to live and work in Vienna, the imperial capital where he built his freelance career, wrote his greatest operas, and died in 1791. The city's musical market, court, and burial customs shaped both his life and the legends around his death.

rabbitHole.label: Napoleon
rabbitHole.reason: Mozart died in 1791 as revolutionary upheaval was beginning to remake Europe, the world Napoleon would soon dominate. 'The Marriage of Figaro,' with its servants outwitting their masters, caught the social tensions of the age that the revolutions would bring to a head.

rabbitHole.label: The Beatles
rabbitHole.reason: Like Mozart, the Beatles became a byword for melodic genius and astonishing productivity in a short span, their gift mythologized as effortless. The comparison sharpens how popular memory turns extraordinary craft into a story of natural, almost magical talent.

reference.author: Solomon, Maynard
reference.title: Mozart: A Life
reference.annotation: The most influential modern English-language biography, reading the life and the music together and dismantling the sentimental image of the effortless child, with particular attention to the fraught relationship with Leopold. Contested for how far its psychological inferences run ahead of the evidence, yet no later account has displaced it as the baseline the rest of the field argues with.

reference.author: Deutsch, Otto Erich
reference.title: Mozart: A Documentary Biography
reference.annotation: The standard assembly of contemporary documents on Mozart's life: letters, reviews, contracts, and accounts. The indispensable primary source against which every interpretive narrative, including the death-myths, must be checked, and the foundation on which modern documentary scholarship rests.

reference.author: Landon, H.C. Robbins
reference.title: 1791: Mozart's Last Year
reference.annotation: The detailed reconstruction of Mozart's final year, the Requiem commission, the illness, and the death and burial. The best single account for separating the documented 1791 from the accumulated legend of poisoning and the pauper's grave, weighing the evidence soberly where it is thin.

reference.author: Eisen, Cliff, and Keefe, Simon P. (eds.)
reference.title: The Cambridge Mozart Encyclopedia
reference.annotation: A comprehensive reference assembling current scholarship across the life, works, contexts, and reception, with careful entries scrutinizing which famous anecdotes and documents are authentic. Valuable for checking specific claims against the state of the field rather than against the legend.

reference.author: Heartz, Daniel
reference.title: Mozart, Haydn and Early Beethoven, 1781–1802
reference.annotation: Situates Mozart within the wider Classical style as a shared European achievement alongside Haydn and the young Beethoven, treating the idiom historically rather than as a solitary miracle. Foundational for understanding what 'Classical' meant as a common practice and where Mozart stood within it.

reference.author: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, and Mozart, Leopold
reference.title: The Letters of Mozart and His Family
reference.annotation: The family correspondence, edited and translated by Emily Anderson, that documents the touring years, the break with Salzburg, and the Vienna career in the participants' own words. Essential primary material, though the letters are shaped by their writers' aims and cannot be read as transparent fact.

reference.author: Gutman, Robert W.
reference.title: Mozart: A Cultural Biography
reference.annotation: A wide-ranging account that places Mozart in the social, political, and intellectual world of his time, useful for the Enlightenment context of his career and the patronage system he sought to escape. A counterweight to narrowly biographical readings, though long and discursive.

commerce.author: Gutman, Robert W.
commerce.title: Mozart: A Cultural Biography
commerce.note: For the newcomer who wants Mozart placed in his world rather than reduced to a list of masterpieces. It is long but readable, and it supplies the social and political setting that makes the career intelligible, no musical training required.

commerce.author: Rosselli, John
commerce.title: The Life of Mozart
commerce.note: The right opening move for a reader who wants a short, reliable life without legend. Rosselli is concise and skeptical, covering the prodigy years, the Vienna career, and the death in a single brief volume you can finish before a single opera.

commerce.author: Solomon, Maynard
commerce.title: Mozart: A Life
commerce.note: For the reader ready to engage the life seriously. Solomon's psychologically searching biography is demanding but accessible, and it is the single best book for understanding how the legend of the effortless child was built and why it misleads.

commerce.author: Landon, H.C. Robbins
commerce.title: 1791: Mozart's Last Year
commerce.note: For the reader who wants to know what actually happened at the end. Landon reconstructs the final year, the Requiem, and the burial in detail, and it is the best guide to separating the documented death from the poisoning and pauper's-grave myths.

commerce.author: Heartz, Daniel
commerce.title: Mozart, Haydn and Early Beethoven, 1781–1802
commerce.note: For the reader ready to engage the music as a shared Classical practice. Heartz situates Mozart among his peers with real analytical depth, and it is the serious next step for someone who wants to understand the style rather than just the man.

commerce.author: Deutsch, Otto Erich
commerce.title: Mozart: A Documentary Biography
commerce.note: For the reader who wants the sources themselves. This collection of letters, reviews, and records lets you meet the working professional directly and is where to go when you want to check a claim against the evidence rather than take it on trust.

popularCulture.title: Amadeus (1984, dir. Miloš Forman)
popularCulture.description: This film made Mozart's story famous for millions, told through the jealous eyes of the composer Salieri, who claims to have helped kill him. The music and the drama are glorious. But the poisoning is invented, and the real Mozart was not a giggling fool. The movie fixed a gripping legend in people's minds as if it were history.

popularCulture.title: Eine kleine Nachtmusik as the sound of 'elegant' music
popularCulture.description: The opening of this serenade is everywhere, in ads, ringtones, and films, used as shorthand for old, fancy, classical music. Most people who can hum it never learn it is Mozart, or that it is one small, light piece. The fame is real, but it shrinks a vast body of work down to a single pretty tune.

popularCulture.title: Calling a gifted child 'a Mozart'
popularCulture.description: When people call a talented kid 'a little Mozart,' they mean a born genius who needs no training. The phrase captures real awe at early talent. But it hides the truth about the real Mozart, who was drilled hard by his father from his earliest years. The label turns years of fierce practice into a story of pure, effortless magic.

popularCulture.title: The 'Mozart effect' and playing music to babies
popularCulture.description: A 1990s claim said hearing Mozart makes you smarter, which sent parents rushing to play his music to babies. The original study was small and narrow, and the big promises did not hold up. The idea stuck anyway. It shows how Mozart's name became a kind of magic word for intelligence, far beyond anything the music itself does.

popularCulture.title: The unfinished Requiem and its legend
popularCulture.description: Mozart died while writing his Requiem, a mass for the dead, and someone else finished it. The story of a dying man composing his own death-music is haunting, and it feeds the myths about his end. The piece is beautiful and real. But the most dramatic tales around it, of a mysterious stranger and a poisoning, are far more story than fact.

popularCulture.title: The Magic Flute on screen (Bergman, 1975, and later versions)
popularCulture.description: Ingmar Bergman filmed Mozart's fairy-tale opera as warm, playful theater, and others have reset it in new times and places. These versions carry the opera's mix of comedy, wonder, and ritual to people who would never enter an opera house. They also smooth over the strange, dated parts of the old story, keeping the magic and quietly dropping the rest.
